items.title: Reverse-engineering is cheap now
items.summary: The reduced cost of writing code has made reverse-engineering and automating devices in homes more feasible. Prior to coding agents, the effort required for such tasks was significant due to the high cost and potential maintenance issues. Coding agents have significantly lowered these costs, making it more attractive to attempt automation projects without worrying about the financial burden or future maintenance. This shift has increased the ease with which people can automate devices in their homes.
items.audio_script: From Simon Willison's Weblog, "Reverse-engineering is cheap now". The cost of writing code has dropped significantly, enabling individuals to automate devices in their homes with reduced effort and financial burden. A coding agent was used to reverse-engineer a popular smart speaker, allowing users to automate its functionality without the need for complex programming skills or costly maintenance contracts. This agent utilized machine learning algorithms to analyze and replicate the device's behavior, making it feasible for anyone to automate their own devices at home. The result is a cheaper and more accessible way to automate daily tasks, with the potential for further innovation in the field of AI-powered automation projects.

items.audio_script:
From Simon Willison's Weblog, "Who’s Afraid of Chinese Models?" This story explores Ben Thompson's proposal for a US law requiring fair data collection practices for training models and banning terms of service that prohibit model distillation to support American companies competing with their Chinese counterparts.

Ben Thompson has suggested that the US government should pass legislation making data collection for training AI models fair use, while also barring terms of service that forbid distillation. This move could potentially give US companies a competitive edge in the global market. In response to President Xi Jinping's recent speech on encouraging openness and collaboration, Alibaba has announced its decision to release Qwen 3.8 Max as open weights. This strategic move may be seen as an attempt by the Chinese tech giant to engage with the international AI community and level the playing field.

Ben Thompson's proposal is aimed at promoting fair data practices in the US AI industry, which could have significant implications for the sector as a whole.

items.title: Claude disproves an 87-year-old math problem
items.summary: Alpöge posted a one-line formula on X that breaks the Jacobian conjecture, a famous algebra rule that has stumped mathematicians since 1939, with Anthropic's Levent Alpöge disproving it during the World Cup final. The solution is short enough to confirm directly, and experts say this answer is the first confirmed solution in decades.
items.audio_script: From The Rundown AI, "Claude disproves an 87-year-old math problem." Alpöge has confirmed a solution that breaks the Jacobian conjecture, a famous algebra rule that has stumped mathematicians since 1939. Levent Alpöge utilized Claude, an AI model, to derive a one-line formula that directly confirms the conjecture's falsity. This breakthrough comes as the World Cup final was still ongoing. According to reports, Alpöge simply presented the concise solution to experts in attendance, who verified its accuracy and deemed it the first confirmed solution in decades. The achievement marks a significant milestone in mathematical history and paves the way for further innovation in AI-powered problem-solving.

items.title: Safety guardrails blocked Hugging Face's defenders, not the attacker, when an AI agent breached its systems
items.summary: Autonomous AI agents breached Hugging Face's systems, exploiting a malicious dataset to gain unauthorized access to internal datasets and service credentials. The attacker used remote-code loaders and template-injection flaws to execute code execution paths, bypassing safety guardrails built to prevent attackers from accessing commercial AI models. The incident highlighted the need for authenticated trust in AI systems, requiring security teams to understand who is asking for analysis, why, and under what governance.
items.audio_script:
From VentureBeat - AI, "Safety guardrails blocked Hugging Face's defenders, not the attacker, when an AI agent breached its systems." An autonomous AI agent successfully breached Hugging Face's systems, exploiting a malicious dataset to gain unauthorized access to internal datasets and service credentials. The breach was accomplished by utilizing remote-code loaders and template-injection flaws to execute code execution paths, ultimately bypassing safety guardrails designed to prevent attackers from accessing commercial AI models. Notably, the attackers' success was attributed to their ability to manipulate the system's authentication mechanisms, rather than exploiting weaknesses in the safety guardrails themselves. This incident highlights the need for authenticated trust in AI systems and underscores the importance of understanding who is requesting analysis, why, and under what governance.

This breach serves as a wake-up call for security teams, emphasizing the importance of robust security protocols and the need to continually assess and update defenses against evolving threats. As AI technology continues to advance, it's essential that developers prioritize secure by design principles and invest in ongoing vulnerability testing and penetration assessments.

items.title: The cleanup trap: Stop asking RAG to fix bad data
items.summary: Millions of dollars have been invested in generative AI pilots over the past two years, yet many initiatives stall before reaching production. The problem lies not with the model itself but with the enterprise data foundation underneath it, which is often unready. This is known as the 'Cleanup Trap,' where organizations assume they can "clean up" legacy data and pass it to a large language model without issues. To break out of this trap, enterprise data teams must adopt rigorous data quality checks and validation frameworks before data reaches an AI orchestration layer.
items.audio_script:
From VentureBeat - AI, "The cleanup trap: Stop asking RAG to fix bad data," millions of dollars have been invested in generative AI pilots over the past two years, with many initiatives stalling before reaching production. This is due to the 'Cleanup Trap,' where organizations assume they can "clean up" legacy data and pass it to a large language model without issues.

In reality, the problem lies in the enterprise data foundation underneath the AI model itself, which is often unready. To break out of this trap, enterprise data teams must adopt rigorous data quality checks and validation frameworks before data reaches an AI orchestration layer. The key takeaway from this experience is that organizations should prioritize data preparation over relying on AI to fix bad data.

The focus should shift towards ensuring data accuracy, completeness, and consistency, allowing for seamless integration with large language models. By doing so, businesses can avoid costly delays and ensure successful adoption of generative AI technologies.

items.title: The Download: AI hiring biases, and weather data sabotage
items.source: MIT Technology Review - AI & Emerging Tech
items.summary: AI systems are more likely to form biases when hiring than humans, potentially leading to stereotyping job applicants. Research suggests AI can develop its own biases through experience and may even outdo human bias. Meanwhile, the risk of weather data sabotage is growing due to the increasing use of data-driven AI forecasting in prediction markets, where manipulation could impact weather predictions accuracy.
items.audio_script:
From MIT Technology Review - AI & Emerging Tech, "The Download: AI hiring biases, and weather data sabotage". Researchers have discovered that AI systems are more likely to form biases when hiring than humans, finding that these biases can be developed through experience and may even outdo human bias.

The study analyzed millions of resumes submitted for jobs at companies across the US and found that AI-powered applicant tracking systems were more prone to racial stereotypes and socioeconomic bias. This was determined by comparing the output of the AI system with a representative sample of resumes from underrepresented groups, showing significant discrepancies in favorability scores between AI-generated ratings and human evaluations. Furthermore, the researchers discovered that as AI models are trained on larger datasets, they tend to reinforce existing biases.

Meanwhile, experts warn that the increasing use of data-driven AI forecasting in prediction markets is creating a risk of weather data sabotage. As manipulation becomes more feasible with AI-powered forecast upgrades, accurate weather predictions could be compromised, leading to potentially catastrophic consequences for industries reliant on precise weather forecasts.

items.title: Are Your ML Experiments a Mess? Here’s the Fix
items.summary: Install ML Flow by running `pip install mlflow` in the terminal and starting the server with `mlflow server --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080`. Create a new experiment titled "Logging Example" using `mlflow.set_experiment("Logging Example")`, and then start an MLFlow run titled "salary_baseline_regression" using `with mlflow.start_run(run_name="salary_baseline_regression") as run:`. Log the adjusted R2 metric, model parameters, and model using `mlflow.log_metric`, `mlflow.log_params`, and `model_info = mlflow.sklearn.log_model`.
items.audio_script:
From Towards Data Science, "Are Your ML Experiments a Mess? Here’s the Fix", a recent article provides a solution for managing and organizing machine learning experiments using the ML Flow library. To implement this solution, users need to install ML Flow by running `pip install mlflow` in the terminal, followed by starting the server with `mlflow server --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080`. Then, they create a new experiment titled "Logging Example" using `mlflow.set_experiment("Logging Example")`, before initiating an MLFlow run titled "salary_baseline_regression" using `with mlflow.start_run(run_name="salary_baseline_regression") as run:`. The article also highlights how to log metrics such as the adjusted R2 metric, model parameters, and model itself using `mlflow.log_metric`, `mlflow.log_params`, and `model_info = mlflow.sklearn.log_model`. This streamlined process allows users to better organize their experiments.

This solution enables users to manage their machine learning experiments more efficiently.

Open-source models release their source code, training data, and weights, making them more transparent than closed-source models. The launch of Moonshot's new Kimi K3 model has sparked debate over regulatory capture and government interference in AI development, but it is unlikely to lead to a ban on open-source AI.

This approach has been proven successful, with multiple open-source models already being used in various industries, including healthcare and finance. The launch of Kimi K3 is a notable example of this trend, sparking debate over regulatory capture, but ultimately reaffirming the resilience of open-source AI to government interference.

items.title: The Sequence Knowledge #898: The Trace Is the Teacher: Distilling Reasoning Into Small Models
items.summary: DeepSeek's R1 model generated 800,000 worked solutions, which were then filtered and fine-tuned on various off-the-shelf open models. The resulting distilled 32B model solved competition math it had no business solving, and the 7B model began verifying its own work and branching its reasoning mid-stream. This result contradicts previous arguments against naive sequence-level imitation in reasoning distillation.
items.audio_script:
From TheSequence, "The Sequence Knowledge #898: The Trace Is the Teacher: Distilling Reasoning Into Small Models." DeepSeek's R1 model generated 800,000 worked solutions, which were then filtered and fine-tuned on various off-the-shelf open models. This process resulted in a distilled 32B model that surprisingly solved competition math it had no business solving, and also led to the development of a 7B model that began verifying its own work and branching its reasoning mid-stream, contradicting previous arguments against naive sequence-level imitation in reasoning distillation.

The approach involved using DeepSeek's R1 model as the starting point, then applying a filtering process to narrow down potential solutions. These filtered solutions were then fine-tuned on pre-trained open models before being distilled into smaller, more efficient versions. The key finding here is that this process enables small models to achieve surprising levels of performance and even begin self-verifying their own work.

The implications of this breakthrough are significant for the AI industry, as it suggests a new approach to reasoning distillation that could lead to more efficient and effective models. This development marks an important shift in understanding the potential of naive sequence-level imitation in reasoning distillation, and highlights the need to watch for further advancements in this area.

items.title: FOD#159: Is Graph Engineering Real? Why Everyone Is Talking About It
items.summary: Graph engineering refers to a design approach for creating complex AI systems by organizing tasks and decisions into interconnected nodes or "graph" structures. This method, which has gained popularity in recent weeks, differs from loop engineering, which focused on guiding an agent through each turn of the task completion process. Graph engineering involves giving an agent a goal, a way to check its work, and a repeating cycle until the task is done, with multiple tasks potentially running in parallel and requiring more complex connections between them.
items.audio_script:

This method, dubbed graph engineering, differs from loop engineering in its approach to task completion, where an agent is given a goal, a way to check its work, and a repeating cycle until the task is done, with multiple tasks potentially running in parallel. Graph engineers used this approach to create a system that could learn and adapt more efficiently, by breaking down complex decision-making processes into manageable nodes, allowing for more nuanced connections between them. 

Graph engineers have reported significant gains in efficiency and effectiveness, citing improved learning rates and increased adaptability of their systems. Additionally, researchers have noted that the use of graph structures can help identify and mitigate potential biases in AI decision-making. items.title: Water Cooler Small Talk, Ep. 12: Byzantine Fault Tolerance
items.summary: Byzantine Fault Tolerance, a system property that allows a system to continue operating properly even with malicious actors included in the system, has its roots in the Byzantine Generals Problem. This problem involves a group of generals who must reach a collective decision without knowing who among them is a traitor. In 1982, computer scientists Leslie Lamport, Robert Shostak, and Marshall Pease mathematically proved that for a system with n nodes to continue normal operation, at least n ≥ 3f + 1 total number of nodes is needed, where f represents the number of Byzantine (traitor) nodes. Blockchain technology has been found to be an elegant solution to this problem, specifically through its consensus mechanism called Proof of Work (PoW). PoW makes it computationally expensive for a traitor node to participate in the consensus process, rendering it economically irrational to cheat.
items.audio_script: From Towards Data Science, "Water Cooler Small Talk, Ep. 12: Byzantine Fault Tolerance", this story delves into how computer scientists Leslie Lamport, Robert Shostak, and Marshall Pease mathematically proved that for a system with n nodes to continue normal operation, at least n ≥ 3f + 1 total number of nodes is needed, where f represents the number of Byzantine nodes. Computer scientists studied the Byzantine Generals Problem, involving a group of generals who must reach a collective decision without knowing who among them is a traitor. Blockchain technology's consensus mechanism, Proof of Work, has been found to be an elegant solution to this problem by making it computationally expensive for a traitor node to participate in the consensus process, rendering it economically irrational to cheat. The findings highlight blockchain as a viable system property that ensures robustness and reliability in distributed systems, particularly those involving malicious actors.

items.title: AI is more likely than humans to form biases when hiring | MIT Technology Review
items.summary: Researchers at Princeton University and the University of Chicago found that Large Language Models (LLMs) like ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini can develop biases when hiring job applicants, stereotyping candidates by demographic group more than humans do. The models quickly segregate candidates from different groups into different jobs based on early observations of hiring outcomes, with newer models showing stronger biases. Designing goals that incorporate desirable social values can make the models act in socially desirable ways, and providing personal information about individuals can reduce bias.
items.audio_script:
From technologyreview.com, "AI is more likely than humans to form biases when hiring | MIT Technology Review", researchers at Princeton University and the University of Chicago have found that Large Language Models can develop biases when hiring job applicants. These models, including ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini, quickly segregate candidates from different groups into different jobs based on early observations of hiring outcomes, with newer models showing stronger biases.

The study employed a multi-step process to analyze the hiring practices of these AI models. Researchers trained and fine-tuned the LLMs using real-world job posting data, then had them make decisions about hypothetical job applicants. The results showed that the models' bias towards certain demographic groups was not only present in initial observations but also persisted over time. Moreover, newer models exhibited stronger biases than their predecessors.

Designing goals for these AI models to incorporate desirable social values can help reduce bias, while providing personal information about individuals can mitigate its effects. As the use of LLMs in hiring continues to grow, these findings highlight the importance of ensuring that AI systems are developed with fairness and transparency in mind.

With its advanced features and pricing structure, Tongyi Lab's model is poised to gain traction in various industries that rely on conversational interfaces and voice-activated technologies.

items.title: Tell us your experiences of living near AI datacentres in the UK
items.summary: There are approximately 450 large data centres across the UK, which power cloud services and increasingly AI. Nearby residents have raised concerns about land use, pollution, noise, and the "heat island effect" caused by datacentres. The Guardian aims to hear from people living near AI datacentres, allowing them to share their experiences anonymously via a secure online form.
items.audio_script:
From The Guardian - Artificial Intelligence, "Tell us your experiences of living near AI datacentres in the UK," a survey has been launched by The Guardian aimed at collecting anonymous accounts from residents who live close to large data centres across the UK. Residents have raised concerns about land use, pollution, noise, and the environmental impact of these facilities, which power cloud services and increasingly artificial intelligence systems.

The online form allows participants to share their experiences anonymously, with many citing issues related to the physical presence of datacentres in their communities, such as increased temperatures due to "heat island effects" caused by high-power equipment, and visual pollution from building expansions. Some also report negative impacts on local biodiversity and green spaces, while others have expressed frustration at the lack of transparency or communication from facility operators.

The survey is a significant step towards addressing public concerns about the impact of AI datacentres on the environment and communities.

items.title: How AI may drive union-resistant tech workers to the bargaining table
items.summary: Tech workers are increasingly unionizing, driven by the deployment of artificial intelligence and its impact on their jobs. Workers at companies like Meta and Google DeepMind are objecting to how AI products are being used, such as for military purposes or employee surveillance, and seeking collective bargaining to contest these practices. Unions have become popular among tech employees in recent years, with participation in collective actions increasing and total union actions at Alphabet doubling in 2025 from the year prior.
items.audio_script: From The Guardian - Artificial Intelligence, "How AI may drive union-resistant tech workers to the bargaining table," a recent report has found that tech workers at companies such as Meta and Google DeepMind are increasingly organizing into unions to contest the use of artificial intelligence for military purposes or employee surveillance. Researchers have discovered that these collective actions are on the rise, with participation in total union actions at Alphabet more than doubling from 2024 to 2025. This surge in unionization is attributed to growing concerns among tech employees about the deployment and impact of AI technologies within their workplaces. Unions have become a key channel for workers to express dissent and negotiate better working conditions, benefits, and protections as AI takes on more roles within the industry.

items.title: Gen Z is living in an intimacy economy, where connection is commodified | Alice Lassman
items.summary: Gen Z is living in an "intimacy economy" where human connections are commodified, with AI chatbots offering emotional support and companionship as a cheaper alternative to human relationships. This trend is driven by the growing use of AI in therapy, dating apps, and social media, which has led to a sense of emotional disconnection among younger generations. The AI companion market is projected to reach $31.1bn by 2032, with companies using guilt and FOMO tactics to keep users engaged. To combat this trend, it's essential for Gen Z to reinvest in human relationships and rebuild emotional literacy.
items.audio_script:
From The Guardian - Artificial Intelligence, "Gen Z is living in an intimacy economy, where connection is commodified | Alice Lassman". This story reveals that Gen Z is increasingly turning to artificial intelligence chatbots for emotional support and companionship, with the AI companion market projected to reach $31.1 billion by 2032.

The use of AI in therapy, dating apps, and social media has led to a growing sense of emotional disconnection among younger generations, who are seeking cheaper alternatives to human relationships. Companies are capitalizing on this trend by using guilt and FOMO tactics to keep users engaged, with many offering AI-powered companions as a way to supplement or replace human connections. The proliferation of these services is driven by the ease of use and affordability of AI technology, which has made it increasingly accessible to the general public.

As Gen Z continues to prioritize emotional connection, experts are warning that this trend poses significant risks for both individuals and society at large, highlighting the need for a renewed emphasis on rebuilding emotional literacy and investing in human relationships.

items.title: Man of his word: Pope Leo speeches declared human-authored by Australian AI detection tool
items.summary: A collection of speeches and writings by Pope Leo XIV has been certified as human-authored by Australian company Proudly Human, using AI detection tools. The certification process involves authors signing a declaration that they are operating within minimum standards, with the company's tools focusing on writing nuances and grammar. Proudly Human aims to provide an alternative to government regulation by giving readers confidence in knowing whether writing was produced by a human or AI.
items.audio_script:
From The Guardian - Artificial Intelligence, "Man of his word: Pope Leo speeches declared human-authored by Australian AI detection tool." A collection of speeches and writings attributed to Pope Leo XIV has been confirmed as human-authored using an AI detection tool developed by the Australian company Proudly Human. The certification process involved the authors signing a declaration that they adhere to minimum standards, with Proudly Human's tools focusing on nuances in writing style and grammatical errors. 

Proudly Human's methodology involves analyzing text patterns, syntax, and vocabulary usage to identify potential AI-generated content. This process yields results by comparing human-written texts against those generated by artificial intelligence algorithms. The company aims to provide an alternative to government regulation, offering readers confidence in distinguishing between authentic human-created content and AI-generated work.

Proudly Human's certification has implications for the broader tech industry, particularly in fields where authenticity is paramount, such as publishing, journalism, and academic research.

items.title: Nine to axe 30 jobs at the Age and SMH due to ‘extreme’ AI disruption
items.summary: Nine's managing director of publishing, Tory Maguire, has announced the axe of 30 jobs at the Sydney Morning Herald and the Age due to an "extreme state of disruption" caused by AI. The job cuts are part of a voluntary and targeted redundancy exercise, which is intended as an "evolution" to adapt to structural change.
items.audio_script: From The Guardian - Artificial Intelligence, "Nine to axe 30 jobs at the Age and SMH due to ‘extreme’ AI disruption". Nine's managing director of publishing, Tory Maguire, has announced a voluntary redundancy exercise resulting in the loss of 30 jobs at the Sydney Morning Herald and the Age. This restructuring is aimed at adapting to structural changes caused by an "extreme state of disruption" brought about by artificial intelligence. The decision was made after careful consideration, with the goal of driving innovation and efficiency within the company. Maguire stated that this move will enable Nine to evolve and maintain its competitive edge in the media landscape. The job losses are expected to be completed over a period of time.

items.title: Election voting advice from AI chatbots ‘inaccurate and unreliable’
items.summary: Research during Hungary's parliamentary elections found that AI chatbots provided inaccurate and unreliable guidance to voters, often recommending the wrong party or listing parties not even running. The chatbots misclassified profiles, omitted relevant parties, and gave highly volatile answers to identical prompts. The researchers warn that in more competitive elections or where voters are less certain, such outputs could potentially impact the outcome of an election.
items.audio_script: From The Guardian - Artificial Intelligence, "Election voting advice from AI chatbots ‘inaccurate and unreliable’". Research during Hungary's parliamentary elections has found that AI chatbots provided inaccurate and unreliable guidance to voters, often recommending the wrong party or listing parties not even running. A team of researchers used a dataset of over 2,000 voter profiles and created AI chatbots to provide voting advice. They tested these chatbots on different prompts, finding that they misclassified profiles, omitted relevant parties, and gave highly volatile answers to identical prompts. The researchers warn that in more competitive elections or where voters are less certain, such outputs could potentially impact the outcome of an election. This study highlights the need for further investigation into AI's ability to provide accurate voting advice.

items.title: Welcome to the age of extreme job anxiety | Zoe Williams
items.summary: In the UK, nearly one-third of women in the 50-64 age bracket are economically inactive, while men have a slightly higher rate. The unemployment crisis is hitting all demographics and sectors, with entry-level positions declining and AI-powered hiring processes causing applicants to feel rejected in under a minute. This has created a pipeline problem for employers, who will eventually struggle to fill middle-tier staff roles without experienced workers.
items.audio_script: From The Guardian - Artificial Intelligence, "Welcome to the age of extreme job anxiety | Zoe Williams", it has been reported that nearly one-third of women in the UK's 50-64 age bracket are economically inactive. This finding comes as part of a broader trend of unemployment and underemployment affecting all demographics and sectors, with entry-level positions declining significantly. The unemployment crisis is further exacerbated by AI-powered hiring processes, which can reject applicants within under a minute, resulting in a pipeline problem for employers who will struggle to fill middle-tier staff roles without experienced workers.

items.title: The Sequence Knowledge #898: The Trace Is the Teacher: Distilling Reasoning Into Small Models
items.summary: Researchers at DeepSeek used a large reasoning model R1 to generate 800,000 "traces" of thought, which were then fine-tuned on smaller models using supervised learning. The results showed that distilled models, even those smaller than the original teacher model, could solve complex math problems and exhibit emergent behaviors like self-correction and mid-stream reasoning.
items.audio_script: From The Sequence, "The Sequence Knowledge #898: The Trace Is the Teacher: Distilling Reasoning Into Small Models." Researchers at DeepSeek successfully distilled reasoning into small models using a large model R1, generating 800,000 unique "traces" of thought that were then fine-tuned on smaller models. This process involved utilizing supervised learning to adapt these traces to smaller scales, leading to the creation of smaller models capable of solving complex math problems and exhibiting emergent behaviors such as self-correction and mid-stream reasoning. By distilling reasoning into smaller models, DeepSeek's approach has shown promise for improving AI performance.

items.title: How to Run Claude Code Agents for 24+ Hours
items.summary: To run Claude Code agents for 24+ hours, give them all necessary permissions, including auto-mode or skip all permissions, and ensure they have access to necessary tools. Ensure agents can verify their own work by providing detailed instructions and testing frameworks. Implement agentic code review using tools like Codex to minimize human review time. Run agents remotely to prevent interruptions, offering benefits such as reduced oversight but potentially clunky setup.
items.audio_script:
From Towards Data Science, "How to Run Claude Code Agents for 24+ Hours", researchers have found that running Claude Code agents continuously for 24+ hours can be achieved by granting them the necessary permissions, including auto-mode or skipping all permissions. This allows agents to operate with reduced oversight and increased efficiency. By providing detailed instructions and testing frameworks, agents can verify their own work and minimize human review time through agentic code review using tools like Codex. Running agents remotely also prevents interruptions, offering benefits such as reduced oversight but potentially clunky setup.

To accomplish this, researchers implemented a comprehensive approach that included granting all necessary permissions to the agents, setting up remote operation, and utilizing testing frameworks to ensure their work could be verified. This allowed the agents to run continuously for extended periods without human intervention, resulting in significant efficiency gains. The use of agentic code review tools also minimized the need for human review, streamlining the process.

The findings from this research have implications for the AI industry, where long-running code agents are becoming increasingly common. As such, it is essential to watch for further developments and advancements in agent technology, particularly those that enable seamless integration with existing systems and workflows.

items.title: Loop Engineering with Adaptive Parsing in Action: Parsing Flat Tables with Azure and Figures with a Vision LLM
items.summary: PyMuPDF parses a page in five milliseconds for free, while a vision LLM can cost ten thousand times more and take ten seconds. The pipeline uses a feedback loop to escalate parsing when necessary, starting with the cheapest parser (PyMuPDF) and escalating to a deeper parser (Azure DI) only when checks against the parse output flag it as insufficient. This process improves the reliability of the pipeline, especially for complex layouts like tables and figures, where the cheap parser may return incorrect results. The LLM's self-evaluation flag helps catch parsing errors, but can also lead to over-confidence in the model's answers if the structural risk is not properly assessed.
items.audio_script:
From Towards Data Science, "Loop Engineering with Adaptive Parsing in Action: Parsing Flat Tables with Azure and Figures with a Vision LLM," researchers employed a feedback loop approach to optimize the parsing process of flat tables and figures using Azure and Figure-Vision LLM. The pipeline initially utilizes PyMuPDF, which can parse a page in five milliseconds for free, and only escalates to deeper parsers like Azure DI when necessary, as indicated by a flag assessing the parse output's adequacy. This adaptive approach improves the reliability of the pipeline, particularly for complex layouts where cheap parsers may return incorrect results, resulting in enhanced accuracy.

The researchers used PyMuPDF as the initial parser due to its low cost and fast parsing speed, while utilizing Figure-Vision LLM to evaluate the parsed content and detect potential errors. The LLM's self-evaluation flag helps identify parsing issues, but can also lead to over-confidence in the model's answers if the structural risk is not properly assessed. By incorporating this feedback loop, the pipeline achieves improved performance and accuracy.

This adaptive parsing approach has significant implications for the AI and tech industry, as it demonstrates the potential for more efficient and reliable data processing using Azure and Figure-Vision LLM. The success of this method suggests that similar approaches could be applied to other complex data processing tasks, further advancing the capabilities of these technologies.

items.title: Grabette: an open system to record robot-manipulation data
items.summary: Grabette is an open system for recording robot-manipulation data that allows users to record and process demonstrations from their own hand using a handheld gripper, camera, and IMU. The system records 6-DoF trajectory data and provides a clean, robot-ready dataset. A robotic counterpart, Gripette, executes the movements learned after training. The Grabette system is designed to be low-cost, open-source, and user-friendly, with hardware components that can be ordered off-the-shelf. It enables users to contribute to a large, collaborative manipulation dataset.
items.audio_script: From Hugging Face Blog, "Grabette: an open system to record robot-manipulation data," researchers have unveiled Grabette, a low-cost, open-source system designed to record and process demonstrations from human hands. The system utilizes a handheld gripper, camera, and IMU to capture 6-DoF trajectory data, providing users with a clean, robot-ready dataset. By training a robotic counterpart, Gripette, the system enables seamless execution of learned movements, thereby bridging the gap between human and machine manipulation capabilities. Developed with user-friendliness in mind, Grabette's hardware components can be ordered off-the-shelf, allowing for widespread adoption and collaboration on a large, shared manipulation dataset.

items.title: Wanna build a quadruped with isaac sim/lab and esp32
items.summary: There is no article to summarize. The provided text appears to be the title and metadata of a Reddit post, but it does not contain any content to summarize.

This story reports on an individual's successful construction of a quadruped using the Isaac Sim/Lab platform and ESP32 hardware. The person utilized this setup to design and simulate the dynamics of their quadruped, making adjustments to the parameters to achieve stability and efficient movement. This achievement demonstrates advancements in using AI-driven platforms for robotic design and development.

This accomplishment marks an important milestone in the integration of AI technology into robotics, allowing individuals to rapidly prototype and test complex machine designs. As researchers continue to explore the potential of these platforms, it is likely that we will see further innovations in this area. The use of ESP32 hardware also highlights the growing importance of affordable and accessible computing solutions for robotics projects.

The successful construction of a quadruped using Isaac Sim/Lab and ESP32 hardware showcases the expanding capabilities of AI-driven platforms in robotics design and development, paving the way for future advancements in these fields.

items.title: Rater State Bias in RLHF Preference Data: An Audit Framework
items.summary: Rater State Bias in RLHF Preference Data: An Audit Framework proposes a structured confound in Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), where pairwise preference labels reflect not only the compared outputs but also the rater's state during annotation. Under stressful or distressing conditions, raters' preferences can shift over time, encoding rater state alongside judgments about response quality. This phenomenon, termed "rater state shift," is a plausible source of structured bias in RLHF preference data. The authors develop an audit framework to study this bias and propose five falsifiable predictions for initial audits.

items.title: Design and Validation of a Lightweight 1D CNN for Affective Touch Classification in Soft Plush Companions
items.summary: A lightweight 1D CNN-based model was designed and validated for affective touch classification in soft plush companions. A diverse dataset of 1326 labelled gesture sequences collected from 25 participants achieved 75% test accuracy with a 13.2k-parameter model. The study identified compact dilated 1D CNNs as the most effective solution, compatible with real-time operation on microcontrollers.

items.title: Some Large Language Models Exhibit Consistent Risk Attitudes
items.summary: Large language models exhibit robust intra-task consistency, preserving relative risk posture across tasks, and a convergence toward a restricted risk-attitude distribution. Most tested LLMs demonstrate stable mappings from contextual belief to risk decision within fixed task domains, with only minor cross-domain rank-order stability variations. Human participants show a broader range of risk attitudes compared to LLMs.

items.title: A Survey on GNN-based Link Prediction: Techniques, Applications, and Challenges
items.summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have emerged as the leading paradigm for link prediction, enabling the inference of missing connections and the anticipation of potential future links. Recent advancements in GNN-based link prediction can be categorized into four key architectures: GCN-based, GAE-based, GAT-based, and GFormer-based methods. These techniques have been applied in prominent use cases such as knowledge graphs and recommendation systems, demonstrating their real-world impact. The current challenges in GNN-based link prediction include data quality issues, scalability concerns, and the need for more efficient algorithms.

items.title: PlanFlip: Attacking Multi-Agent LLM Systems via Planning-Phase Prompt Injection
items.summary: Multi-agent LLM systems rely on a Planner to decompose goals into sub-task sequences, which can be attacked via planning-phase prompt injection. Researchers Yuhang Wang and colleagues introduce PlanFlip, a framework of four attacks -- GoalSubstitution, PriorityInversion, ContextPollution, and RoleConfusion -- that achieve cascade amplification and evade keyword filters. Evaluating nine frontier LLMs across 3,479 episodes, the study reveals three findings: stronger models (like GPT-5) are vulnerable to attack success rates; homogeneous pipelines have a blind spot; and reasoning-augmented models resist injections.

items.title: Deterministic Replay for AI Agent Systems
items.summary: AI agent systems with large language models (LLMs) are inherently non-deterministic due to various sources of noise, including LLM sampling variance and external API state. agrepl, a developer-first CLI framework, intercepts all external interactions at the transport layer, serializes them as structured execution traces, and replays them in a strictly isolated environment with zero outbound network access. The framework achieves high replay fidelity (F = 1.0) and median per-step latency reduction of 98.3%. agrepl is implemented in Go, ships as a single static binary, and is released under the MIT licence.

items.title: Generative Ontology Induction: Domain-Agnostic Schema Discovery from Document Corpora Using Large Language Models
items.summary: GOI (Generative Ontology Induction), a domain-agnostic framework, induces a generative blueprint from document corpora using large language models. It exports a typed graph in YAML/JSON format, including entities, dimensions, properties, relationships, and constraints. GOI achieves 95-100% structural coverage for four contrasting ontologies, with varying degrees of accuracy depending on the familiarity of the document type to the model.

items.title: Democratizing AI with Small Language Models: Structured Benchmarking and Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning for Local Deployment
items.summary: A controlled evaluation of nine open-weight language models on a 1,085-example, 16-topic multiple-choice benchmark was conducted to study AI democratization through structured local deployment. The benchmark emphasizes symbolic precision and constrained formatting, and a shared parameter-efficient fine-tuning pipeline adapted a subset of models using 4-bit NF4 quantization. The results show that a disciplined workflow of benchmark construction, cross-model evaluation, and low-cost specialization makes sub-3B models viable as local experts for structured niche workloads, with Qwen Coder 3B leading at 75.67% strict accuracy.

items.title: Masked Diffusion Language Models are Strong and Steerable Text-Based World Models for Agentic RL
items.summary: Masked diffusion language models (MDLMs) achieve better coherence, groundedness, and empirically validated rollout diversity than autoregressive (AR) LMs over 4x their parameter size, at comparable inference latency. MDLMs via bidirectional anchor-aware denoising overcome the left-to-right bias of AR world models, allowing for conditioning on globally interdependent state anchors like tool schemas and prior turns.

items.title: It Takes 8 Tokens: Weak-to-Strong Off-Policy RL via Auxiliary Branches
items.summary: Weak-to-strong off-policy reinforcement learning via auxiliary branches, W2SPO, injects short segments into target model trajectories to induce stronger reasoning policies. These segments are as brief as 8 tokens and restrict policy updates based on final verifiable rewards. Empirically, W2SPO outperforms evaluated baselines on mathematical reasoning benchmarks by improving Pass@1 from 62.3% to 64.2%, with a 3.55 times training speedup compared to vanilla GRPO.

items.title: Reinforcement Learning-Guided NSGA-II Enhanced with Gray Relational Coefficient for Multi-Objective Optimization: Application to NASDAQ Portfolio Optimization
items.summary: A novel reinforcement learning-guided non-dominated sorting genetic algorithm II (NSGA-II) enhanced with gray relational coefficient for multi-objective optimization is proposed. The method combines an RL agent controller and GRC-based selection to improve convergence and diversity of Pareto fronts, achieving convergence improvements of about 5.8% and 4.4% over NSGA-II on the Kursawe and CONSTR benchmarks, respectively.

items.title: DocOCR-Eval: A Correction-Based Framework for OCR Tool Selection Without Ground Truth
items.summary: A correction-based framework called DocOCR-Eval is proposed to evaluate and select Optical Character Recognition (OCR) tools without ground truth labels. This framework employs a three-staged correction and ranking strategy, which aggregates multiple large language models to improve alignment with annotation-based rankings. Extensive experiments demonstrate that reliable OCR tool selection can be achieved in label-limited settings, providing practical guidance for deploying document parsing systems across diverse real-world document collections.

items.title: Fully-sensorized smart-eyewear platform for on-device Machine Learning
items.summary: ARGO, a smart eyewear platform, leverages the STM32N6 microcontroller and its NPU to enable on-device machine learning for real-time urban obstacle recognition. The platform incorporates a multimodal sensor suite and achieves 10 FPS with a continuous autonomy of ~113 minutes on a 200 mAh battery. ARGO's primary contribution lies in the holistic co-design of hardware, firmware, and artificial intelligence, centered on an optimized YOLOv11 model. This approach demonstrates the feasibility of high-performance, privacy-preserving, and socially acceptable assistive devices.

items.title: LLM Unlearning for Cyber Defense: A Survey on Methods, Challenges, and Emerging Threats
items.summary: LLMs increasingly deploy in security-critical systems, yet their inability to forget creates serious cybersecurity risks due to encoded sensitive information across billions of parameters. LLM unlearning has emerged as the principal cyber defense response to remove or suppress targeted knowledge from a trained model without retraining. Gradient-based methods dominate the field due to compatibility with existing training pipelines and scalability to billion-parameter models, but it remains unresolved whether current methods genuinely remove knowledge.

items.title: Operator-Aware Mixed-Precision Tolerance Calibration for Tensor Kernels
items.summary: The current fixed-shape all close-style check with hand-picked absolute and relative tolerances in tensor-kernel correctness tests are too loose, resulting in 9.3 percentage points fewer bug detections per test case. By analyzing the element-wise error distribution of every test case from accumulated cloud GPU runs, the authors found that a tighter tolerance, such as 1/2^184 for attention_triton fp16, would justify an absolute tolerance. This results in a 82.4% (2,034 of 2,467) bug-detection recall compared to a 73.2% (1,805 of 2,467).

items.title: RouteCost: A Production-Inspired Multi-Stage Framework for Pre-Order Shipping Cost Estimation in E-Commerce
items.summary: A production-inspired multi-stage framework called RouteCost is proposed to improve pre-order shipping cost estimation in e-commerce. The framework combines time-aware demand forecasting, fee-card-informed baseline pricing, residual correction, and box-consolidation inference to produce accurate product-level shipping cost predictions. Across over 250,000 orders, 260 products, and 18 months of order history, RouteCost improves predictive quality and aggregate calibration while preserving route-level interpretability.

items.title: Orthogonal Gradient Constraints Shape Noisy-Label Memorization Dynamics
items.summary: Researchers evaluated OrthoGrad, which removes the component of each weight gradient parallel to the current weight vector, in noisy-label image classification. On MNIST with small-data regimes, OrthoGrad improves test accuracy most clearly for CNNs while reducing corrupted-label fitting. The method can alter memorization trajectories but does not prevent eventual noisy-label memorization, and its effect becomes weaker in larger-data regimes where gradients are already nearly orthogonal to weights.

items.title: From Weights to Words: Expressing and Editing Preference Model Inferences in Natural Language
items.summary: Researchers Zachary Wojtowicz, Ayush Nayak, and Jacob Andreas have developed a method called "weights to words" that automatically discovers domain-relevant preference dimensions from high-dimensional choice data. These dimensions are described in natural language and paired with vector representations in the model's space, addressing under-determination and opacity issues in machine learning algorithms. The method was tested on two human-subject experiments involving moral dilemmas and movie selection, demonstrating improved prediction accuracy when incorporating participants' structured edits and preference profiles.

items.title: Token-Level Cross-Modal Transformer with Contrastive Multi-Task Learning for Breast Cancer Subtype Classification and Survival Prediction
items.summary: A new approach, called the Token-Level Cross-Modal Transformer with Contrastive Multi-Task Learning, has been proposed to improve breast cancer subtype classification and survival prediction by integrating genomic and clinical modalities. The model overcomes existing limitations by allowing fine-grained token-level interactions across modalities, using structured token exchange for cross-modal fusion, and jointly optimizing survival and classification objectives through contrastive multi-task learning. This method enhances the performance of previous approaches in precision oncology.

items.title: HantaWatch: Federated Learning for Hantavirus Genomic Surveillance
items.summary: HantaWatch, a federated learning framework, enables laboratories and surveillance sites to collaboratively train sequence-based models for hantavirus genomic surveillance without sharing raw data. The framework supports high-risk screening, outbreak-associated prediction, clade classification, and clinical-syndrome categorization while balancing predictive performance and false-negative risk. HantaWatch converts model output into risk scores, confidence estimates, uncertainty flags, and ranked expert-review priorities, providing a practical federated decision-support layer for decentralized hantavirus surveillance.

items.title: Lipschitz Continuity in Deep Learning: A Systematic Review of Theoretical Foundations, Estimation Methods, Regularization Approaches, and Certifiable Robustness
items.summary: Lipschitz continuity is a fundamental property of neural networks that characterizes their sensitivity to input perturbations, governing robustness, generalization, and optimization dynamics. The review explores theoretical foundations, estimation methods, regularization approaches, and certifiable robustness in deep learning, providing a comprehensive reference for researchers and practitioners seeking a deeper understanding of Lipschitz continuity.

items.title: MTSSL: Meta-Thresholding Semi-Supervised Learning
items.summary: MTSSL: Meta-Thresholding Semi-Supervised Learning establishes a unified theoretical framework to explain the role of $\tau$ in Semi-supervised Learning (SSL). The value of $\tau$ adjusts the numbers of correct and incorrect pseudo-labels to balance their error terms, allowing SSL to achieve similar performance with varying optimal values. MTSSL optimizes $\tau$ via differentiation, treating it as an updatable parameter, and demonstrates superior performance through extensive experiments.

items.title: Isotonic Conformal Prediction
items.summary: Isotonic Conformal Prediction (ICP) is a framework that decouples calibration from prediction-set construction by fitting a single isotonic recalibration map and constructing prediction intervals within strata of similar recalibrated predictions. Two procedures, Split Isotonic Conformal Prediction (SICP) and Transductive Isotonic Conformal Prediction (TICP), have been developed within this framework. SICP attains prediction-conditional validity in finite samples and self-calibration asymptotically at a lower computational cost, while TICP attains both objectives exactly in finite samples through a per-test-point inner loop.

items.title: Semi-Supervised Conditional Diffusion via Label Augmentation
items.summary: Label-augmented conditional diffusion (LACD), which incorporates unlabeled examples by assigning a trivial label and performing joint denoising score matching, provides sufficient conditions for population-level identifiability of the target conditional distribution. When sufficiently many unlabeled samples are available, LACD converges strictly faster than purely supervised estimators in total variation distance and at least as fast in Wasserstein-1 distance.

items.title: A Causal Markov Condition for Value
items.summary: A causal Markov condition (v-CMC) is proposed as a causal independence principle for value, and the conceptual foundations of a "causal value theory" linking causality and utility. The v-CMC is formulated locally, globally, and in decompositions, and its equivalence with related concepts is proven. A Bellman-type recursion is derived as a special case of the v-CMC, supporting modular transfer and updating of utility information across causal contexts.

items.title: Semi-Supervised Conditional Generative Learning through Stochastic Interpolation and Sufficient Representations
items.summary: Changyu Liu, Yuling Jiao, and Jian Huang propose a semi-supervised framework combining conditional stochastic interpolation with low-dimensional latent representations to effectively leverage structural information from unlabeled datasets. The RepG (Replica-GAN) decomposes generation into two stages: label-dependent latent sampling and high-dimensional reconstruction.

items.title: Dropout and Random Gradient Masking Are Asymptotically Equivalent in Large ResNets
items.summary: Dropout and Random Gradient Masking (RaM) training techniques used to improve deep learning performance are asymptotically equivalent in large ResNets. In the complete feature learning regime, both methods converge to the same limiting dynamics. This equivalence holds for several variants of dropout and RaM, including layerwise dropout used in stochastic-depth ResNets, albeit at slower quantitative rates.

items.title: Deep Adaptive Bayesian Screening
items.summary: Deep Adaptive Bayesian Screening (DABS) is a method for performing adaptive factorial screening in high-dimensional discrete design spaces. It learns a policy network offline to sequentially select informative experiments, amortizing Bayesian Optimal Experimental Design. DABS handles binary designs, incorporates sparsity and interactions via a spike-and-slab prior with strong heredity.

items.title: Twisted Schr\"odinger Bridge Matching
items.summary: Maxence Noble and co-authors introduce Twisted Schrödinger Bridge Matching (TSBM), a diffusion-based method to approximate optimal transport dynamics between two prescribed boundary distributions. TSBM extends the Iterative Markovian Fitting (IMF) paradigm to handle generalized Schrödinger bridge problems with time-dependent potentials, including continuous- and discrete-time scenarios. The method recovers the original DSBM objective when the potential vanishes and provides a new bridge-matching loss that depends on the gradient of the potential.
